message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Insomnia, poor sleep quality and extremes of sleep duration are associated with COVID-19 infection. This study assessed whether these factors are related to Post-Acute Sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cross-sectional survey of a general population of 24,803 U.S. adults to determine the association of insomnia, poor sleep quality and sleep duration with PASC.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Prevalence rates of PASC among previously COVID-19 infected participants for three definitions of PASC were COPE (21.9%), NICE (38.9%) and RECOVER PASC Score (15.3%). PASC was associated with insomnia in all 3 models in fully adjusted models with adjusted odds ratios (aORs) and 95% confidence intervals (CI) ranging from 1.30 (95% CI: 1.11-1.52, p≤0.05, PASC Score) to 1.52 (95% CI: 1.34-1.71, p≤0.001, (NICE). Poor sleep quality was related to PASC in all models with aORs ranging from 1.77 (95% CI: 1.60-1.97, p≤0.001, NICE) to 2.00 (95% CI: 1.77-2.26, p≤0.001, COPE). Sleep <6 hours was associated with PASC with aORs between 1.59 (95% CI: 1.40-1.80, p≤0.001, PASC Score) to 1.70 (95% CI: 1.53-1.89, p≤0.001, COPE). Sleep
                    <jats:underline>></jats:underline>
                    9 hours was not associated with PASC in any model. Although vaccination with COVID-19 booster decreased the likelihood of developing PASC, it did not attenuate associations between insomnia, poor sleep quality and short sleep duration with PASC in any of the models.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Insomnia, poor sleep quality and short sleep duration are potential risk factors for PASC. Interventions to improve sleep may decrease the development of PASC.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Brief Summary</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Current Knowledge/Study Rationale</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Insomnia, poor sleep quality, and extremes of sleep duration have been associated with a higher likelihood of COVID-19 infection. However, evidence implicating an association with the development of Post-Acute Sequelae of SARS-CoV-2 infection (PASC) is scant.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Study Impact</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>
                      Results indicate that insomnia, poor sleep quality and sleep duration
                      <jats:underline><</jats:underline>
                      6 hours are associated with an increase in the prevalence of PASC among persons who have previously had a COVID-19 infection. The findings provide support for employing interventions to improve sleep as a means to decrease the development of PASC.
                    </jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Sleep and long COVID: Preexisting sleep issues and the risk of PASC in a large general population using 3 different model definitions
